items.q: What is the difference between strength and power?
items.correct: Strength is maximum force; power is force times speed of movement.
items.random_wrong: Strength is for the upper body, power for the lower.
items.why: Students conflate strength and power. A strong person can lift heavy slowly; a powerful athlete generates force QUICKLY (sprinter, shot-putter). Power requires both force and velocity.

items.q: What causes muscle soreness 1-2 days after intense exercise?
items.correct: Microscopic muscle damage (DOMS), not lactic acid.
items.misconception: Lactic acid still in the muscles.
items.why: Students believe the myth that lactic acid causes soreness. Lactate clears within an hour. Delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS) is caused by microtrauma from eccentric contractions, peaking 24-72 hours later.

items.q: Is a tennis serve a closed or open skill?
items.correct: Closed - performed in a predictable, self-paced environment.
items.misconception: Open - because tennis is a fast-paced sport.
items.why: Students judge by the sport, not the moment. The serve happens at the server's pace with a stationary ball - closed. The return of serve is open. Skill classification depends on the SPECIFIC action's environment.
It's SPORT. “FITT” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Tapering. “Overload” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many letters in SPORT principle?
items.why: The real figure is 5 letters. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
